interactions.summary: Jiang explains Caesar's unexpected alliance with Pompey and Crassus, the First Triumvirate, and the exchange that gives Caesar Gaul. He then presents Gaul as the stage where Caesar's myth-making genius becomes legendary through conquest and genocide.

Jiang identifies Caesar's three aims in Gaul: pay debts and fund bread and circus, build the world's most loyal and disciplined army, and create the myth of Caesar as great conqueror through dispatches, Germania, and Britain.
Jiang says the optimates realize Caesar is electorally unbeatable and legally vulnerable, so they move to strip his command and put him on trial; Caesar reads this as war and crosses the Rubicon.

After an audience question, Jiang explains Senate power as unequal despite formal voting equality, then maps the civil-war balance: Caesar controls Rome and Gaul while Pompey and the optimates control the food-rich provinces.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Question is not independently transcribed; Jiang's answer supplies the topic.

speaker_notes.note: Jiang says 'Sure. Oh, that's a great question' before answering about the Senate; the student's wording is not captured.

claims.claim: Caesar forms the First Triumvirate with Pompey and Crassus because all three are blocked by the Senate and can trade political support for each other's goals.
Jiang describes Caesar's Gallic campaign as genocidal and undertaken for personal glory, with Caesar claiming to kill, enslave, and spare a million Gauls each.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's moral diagnosis of Caesar's campaign in this lecture.

claims.claim: The Gallic wars served three purposes in Jiang's account: money for debts and public feasts, a personally loyal army, and a Roman myth of Caesar as great conqueror.
Caesar's dispatches from Gaul functioned as weekly myth production in Rome, turning military reports into public imagination and celebrity.
claims.temporal_scope: Media and myth-making mechanism in this lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang treats Caesar's expeditions into Germania and Britain as imagination-capturing gestures whose symbolic force mattered more than practical military results.
The optimates decide to strip Caesar's command and prosecute him because his military celebrity makes him electorally unbeatable, while his illegal and immoral acts give the Senate a real case.
Senate equality is formal rather than practical: those with highest prestige sit at the front, speak first, set the agenda, and control debate.
claims.temporal_scope: Answer to a student question in this lecture.

claims.claim: At the start of the Caesar-Pompey civil war, Caesar controls Rome and poor Gaul while Pompey and the optimates control Spain, Greece, Anatolia, Syria, and North Africa, including Rome's food supply.

signature_moments.moment: The myth of Caesar as great conqueror is built on a campaign Jiang flatly calls genocide.
signature_moments.source_phrase: he basically committed genocide against the Gauls
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The lecture refuses to separate Caesar's mythic genius from mass violence.

signature_moments.moment: Each week, a soldier reads Caesar's accomplishments in the Roman forum and captures Rome's imagination.
signature_moments.source_phrase: he would read the accomplishments of Caesar of that week
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns conquest into serialized public storytelling.

signature_moments.moment: Caesar's trip to Britain is the Roman equivalent of the moon landing.
signature_moments.source_phrase: equivalent back then of the moon landing
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The image shows why symbolic exploration can matter more than military result.

signature_moments.moment: Caesar constructs a new reality of himself as the great conqueror.
signature_moments.source_phrase: constructing a new reality of Caesar as the great conqueror
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This restates the lecture's myth-maker model in the concrete case of Gaul.

signature_moments.moment: When the Senate moves against him, Caesar answers by declaring war on Rome.
signature_moments.source_phrase: he basically declares war on Rome
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The myth maker crosses from narrative power into open civil war.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The secret alliance of Caesar, Pompey, and Crassus that lets each bypass Senate obstruction and gives Caesar Gaul.
Public feasts and festivals funded by Caesar's Gallic wealth to ingratiate himself with the Roman people.
The northern province where Caesar wages years of war, earns wealth, builds an army, and constructs his conqueror myth.
The boundary Caesar crosses when he effectively declares war on Rome.
A formally collective body whose practical power is concentrated among prestigious front-rank speakers.

uncertainty_notes.note: The transcript renders Rubicon as 'River Cod' in one place; semantic claims normalize to Rubicon.
The student question before Jiang's Senate explanation is not captured, so the interaction is marked exchange with medium confidence rather than a direct question quotation.
